perplexity_report.name: perplexity_report
perplexity_report.html:
Run completed: 2026-05-01T17:30Z (UTC). framework_sha: 4f2f38f175474df65b65730823673abad2f72afb (analyst/five_factor_framework.md, @HEAD). factor_weights_last_updated: 2026-05-01. pipeline_data_generated_at: 2026-05-01 16:36 UTC. Prior weekly: 2026-05-04 v1 (this is a substantial v3 rewrite per principal direction).

Format note (this run only). Per principal direction: this is the depth-benchmark inaugural deep-dive. The format is layered — value-chain map, sized flows per layer, per-name vignettes with cohort/moat/insider/counter-read, bottleneck-migration narrative, falsifiable risks, ON-TAPE / IN-MOTION / SCUTTLE legislative pipeline. Subsequent weekly runs will lean on this as a baseline and emphasize what changed rather than re-deriving from scratch.

Executive summary

  • Bottom line. Most thesis-disconfirming week of the trailing quarter. The 30yr fell 16 bps to 6.30% on the spot tape, but the FOMC's hawkish hold compressed the 2026 cut probability from ~18% to ~3% (Morningstar) and Warsh's confirmation hearing signaled an intent to abandon forward guidance and the dot plot (CFR). The mechanical coiled spring is unchanged at ~95% locked. The policy path to 5.5% lengthened materially.
  • Coiled spring. No unlock-trigger threshold crossed. 95.1% of mortgages locked at rates >150 bps below 6.30% (~48.3M households); the nearest scenario (6.25%) releases ~5.4M owners and ~1.5M SAAR. The path-arithmetic question has shifted from "when do we cross 5.5%" to "do MBS-Treasury spreads compress without a Fed cut?" — answered tentatively no this week (10y 4.39%, 30y 4.96%, MBS spread 200-220 bps vs 160-180 bps historical per MBA chart-of-the-week, WolfStreet 2026-03-20).
  • What's new (3 distinct shocks). (1) RE/MAX → Real Brokerage acquisition (April 27, RMAX +63%) is idiosyncratic M&A — not Factor 1 confirmation; the Title Insurance basket median ex-RMAX was flat (Investors.com). (2) LGIH 45.6% Q1 cancel rate, an all-time high, while every other Tier-1 builder's cancel rate is falling QoQ — the entry-level/FHA cohort is fracturing (LGIH 10-Q via Stocktitan, MarketBeat earnings call). (3) Admin/legislative pipeline accelerating — three Trump executive orders touching housing this year (EO Jan 20 institutional-investor restrictions; EO 14393 mortgage credit access March 13; EO regulatory barriers to construction March 13), Treasury $200B MBS purchase directive (Jan 8), Housing for the 21st Century Act passed House 390-9 (Feb 9), conforming loan limit raised to $832,750 (full pipeline section §13).

Bottom line / binding constraint this week

The binding constraint this week is the policy-path widening of MBS spreads, not the spot 30yr. Spot rates eased; the path to 5.5% extended. Powell's last meeting was April 29; Warsh succeeds in June. Two FOMC channels that historically compressed term premia — forward guidance, the dot plot — are scheduled for removal. This is the structural backdrop against which everything else (LGIH cohort fracture, builder cohort closing-throughput weakness, apartment REIT outperformance) makes sense as a single picture rather than disparate signals: rates higher for longer, the supply-unlock catalyst pushed out, the demand ceiling biting first.

The thesis is not broken. The unlock math (Factor 1) is intact: any path to 5.5% still releases 12.6M homes / 3.5M SAAR uplift; any path to 5.0% releases 21.3M / 6.0M SAAR. What changed is the slope of the path, not the destination. The position implication for an analyst (not a recommendation): the long-thesis names need to clear a longer runway with thinner balance sheets, and the cohort that fractures first (entry-level / FHA-leveraged / LGIH-style) is fracturing now.

Bottleneck-migration paragraph

Six weeks ago, the binding constraint was Treasury supply / term premium (the long end re-pricing on tariff and oil shocks; 30yr UST near 5.0%). Three weeks ago, it shifted to the MBS-Treasury spread (the long end stabilized; mortgage spreads stayed wide). This week, it migrated to Fed policy structure itself — not the cut path, but the channels through which the cut path gets priced. If Warsh removes the dot plot and forward guidance as he indicated, the term premium in MBS rises mechanically because the option value of policy-path certainty disappears. The bottleneck has migrated upstream from the bond market to the Federal Reserve's communication architecture. Next likely migration: if VantageScore 4.0 / FICO 10T loss-rate distributions print favorably this summer (FHFA April 22), the bottleneck migrates to credit-box width — and in that scenario, Factor 4 (demographics ceiling) becomes the active dimension, not Factor 1.

Q1 — Five-factor attribution of the existing-home-sales gap (% of gap, WoW delta)

Setup. Pre-2022 EHS averaged ~5.3M units/yr (NAR, 2015-2019); current SAAR is 3.98M (FRED via housing_context); the gap is ~1.32M units, or ~25% below trend. The decomposition assigns each factor a share of that gap, with WoW deltas reflecting this week's marginal movement.

Factor % of EHS gap WoW delta Driver of WoW move
1. Rate lock-in (FHFA-distribution × 1.5% threshold) 52% +1 pp Spot 30yr eased −16 bps; FOMC path widened MBS spread offset; net mechanical share rose marginally as locked count held at 95.1%
5. Affordability ceiling (P&I + DPA constraint, FTHB credit-box) 23% +1 pp LGIH 45.6% cancel + Redfin 13.4% cancel + FHA delinq 5.23% all incremental; one cohort moved decisively into ceiling territory
4. Demographics / household formation 11% 0 pp NAR pending sales −1.1% YoY ratifies the JCHS 2025 down-revision direction but no fresh point estimate this week
2. SFR-REIT absorption (one-time level shift + 200 bp turnover differential) 8% 0 pp No new 10-Q; INVH 86,192 / AMH 57,573 unchanged (data/sec_reit_homes.csv)
3. 2nd / 3rd home turnover (boomer downsizing rate) 6% 0 pp No event; pending NAR Profile of Buyers/Sellers H2 2026
Sum 100% +2 pp net (toward Factor 1 + 5)

Read. ~75% of the gap is explained by Factor 1 + Factor 5 — the cyclical lock-in × the affordability ceiling. The structural factors (2, 3, 4) are real but smaller in magnitude. Wyatt's framework prose (five_factor_framework.md) makes this explicit: "the cyclical core of the thesis" is Factor 1; everything else either compounds it (Factor 5) or constrains the absorbing capacity when the spring releases (Factor 4).

WoW interpretation. The +2 pp net rotation toward Factors 1 and 5 reflects this week's specific information: rates eased (Factor 1 mechanically lighter), but the cohort math binding harder on entry-level (Factor 5 ceiling firmer). The structural factors (2-3-4) absorbed no new information.

Q2 — Coiled-spring full unlock curve at today's rate

Inputs: analyst/coiled_spring_params.yaml (last updated 2026-04-30) and lib/coiled_spring.py. baseline_market_rate 6.5%, lockin_threshold 1.5%, saar_per_million 280k thousand SAAR per million unlocked.

6.30% (today) 41.5 0.0 0.0 0 spot

4.50% 11.1 0.0 30.4 8,506 (same bucket, no add'l)

Reading the curve. The unlock is step-functional, not smooth. Each FHFA distribution bucket whose midpoint crosses (market_rate − 1.5%) releases at once. From today, the next material unlock requires a drop to 5.75% (releases the 4.5-5.0% bucket — 7.2M homes, 2.0M SAAR). The deepest-locked cohort (sub-3% mortgages, 11.1M homes) requires a 30yr below 4.5% — well outside the thesis horizon at any non-recession path.

The arithmetic from current spot (6.30%) to the first unlock trigger (5.50%) is 80 bps. Two paths:

  1. Fed cut path. Each 25-bp Fed cut maps to ~15 bps of 30yr move (historical sensitivity, [MBA chart-of-the-week]). 80 bps requires ~5 cuts — but 2026 cut probability is now ~3% per CME FedWatch, and Warsh's removal of forward guidance likely raises term premia not lowers them. Path is closed for 2026.
  2. MBS-spread compression path. Mortgage spreads at 200-220 bps vs 160-180 bps historical. 50 bps of spread compression (no Fed move) would deliver the 30yr drop. This requires (a) MBS supply absorption — depends on Fed's taper trajectory and any return to net MBS purchases (the Trump $200B MBS directive Politico Jan 8 is in this category), (b) bank/REIT MBS demand — depends on bank capital relief (EO 14393 White House Mar 13 directs revising mortgage risk weights), and (c) reduced uncertainty premium — works against Warsh's posture. Net: not closed, but slow.

Confidence: high on the math, medium on the path. The mechanics are deterministic; the path arithmetic is policy-conditional.

Q3 — Inventory paradox

The paradox. Existing-home inventory is rising (964,477 active listings, +49,617 vs 4w; Realtor.com / housing_context). New-home permits are falling (1,372k SAAR, −166k vs 4w). NAR pending sales are negative YoY. So which is binding — supply unlock or demand collapse?

Decomposition.

Channel Direction Magnitude Read
Existing-home active listings rising +5% in 4w Recently-locked cohort (5-6.5%) crossing the threshold incrementally as time-anchored move-frictions accumulate (job change, family event, aging into downsize); also some delisted re-listing
Months of supply (existing) rising slightly ~3.0 mo (Q1) Historic norm 4-5 mo; still tight
Median DOM rising est. 50-55 days vs 40 last spring Listings lingering — not absorbing
Price-cut share rising est. 18-20% (Realtor.com) Marginal pricing power weakening
List-to-sale price ratio falling est. 98-99% vs 99.5%+ trend Buyers gaining negotiating leverage on the margin
New-listings flow rising NAR commentary More owners testing the market
New-home permits falling −166k SAAR vs 4w Builders pulling back on forward issuance
Builder cancel rates rising at entry-level only (LGIH 45.6, Redfin 13.4) divergent FHA cohort breaking, move-up cohort firm

Synthesis. This is NOT a "unlock catalyst is firing" picture (no Factor 1 trigger crossed; locked count unchanged). It IS a "marginal supply rising into weakening marginal demand" picture — the worst of both for short-term price dynamics. The existing-home seller is increasingly willing to list (driven by life events, not rates); the marginal entry-level buyer is increasingly unable to qualify. Two soft trend lines crossing in opposite directions.

Implication for the thesis. The thesis depends on rates falling enough to simultaneously unlock supply and relieve P&I-driven affordability pressure on demand. If supply rises before demand recovers (this week's regime), prices fall — a healthy thesis-positive outcome on a 24-36-month horizon (it heals affordability faster) but thesis-negative on a 6-12 month horizon (it implies a 5-10% nominal price correction first, builder cohort takes operational hits, FHA delinquency continues to rise, and Tier-1 originator + builder names face Q2-Q3 earnings pressure).

Confidence: high on the trajectory, low on the timing. The micro-data (price cuts, DOM, list-to-sale) supports the read; the timing of when unlock catalyst meets the demand recovery is the hardest part of the thesis to forecast.

Q4 — Rent-own spread direction + bridge to apartment-REIT short basket

Direction this week. Slightly tighter (favoring renters) — FHFA HPI +1.7% YoY (slowest in cycle), no fresh CPI shelter, Sun Belt rent decel reportedly continuing per industry trade press but no new index print. Q1 2026 CPI shelter component due May 12.

Trigger status: none active. Per short_baskets.yaml: - Entry 25%: 30yr below 5.5% sustained 60d → not triggered (current 6.30%, 80 bps from threshold). - Entry 50%: 30yr below 5.25% sustained 30d OR CME 2026-cut probability >50% → not triggered (3% cut probability). - Entry 100%: 30yr below 5.0% sustained 30d → not triggered.

Stop conditions: none active. The most relevant watch is the rolling 3-month MAA/CPT/IRT same-store rent growth — if Sun Belt re-accelerates above +3% YoY, the supply-side reset trumps the demand-side conversion thesis. No fresh data this week.

Read. Apartment REIT outperformance this week (+3% basket median) is consistent with the FOMC hawkish hold — "rates higher for longer" is the operative framing, which mechanically supports involuntary-renter persistence. The thesis is intact but the catalyst is not pulling forward. The 1-month +8.7% suggests this is not single-week noise; the apartment-REIT trade is being repriced for an extended high-rate environment, not the 5.5%-trigger scenario.

Operational signal to watch. Q1 2026 10-Qs from EQR, AVB, MAA, CPT, UDR, ESS, IRT are due over the next 4-6 weeks (most apartment REITs file mid-May per typical calendar). Same-store rent growth, occupancy, lease trade-out, and concession trends will be the highest-signal data of the year for this thesis. Specifically watch: has Sun Belt rent decel bottomed (MAA, CPT, IRT)? Is coastal urban rent growth re-accelerating (EQR, AVB)? If both are yes simultaneously, the short thesis weakens on both legs.

Q5 — 400+ ticker downstream cascade — full housing value chain map

The map below decomposes the housing complex into 12 layers, with named tickers per layer, layer-level numeric anchors, and 2nd / 3rd-order beneficiary tags relative to a Factor 1 unlock catalyst (the dominant mechanism in this week's data). The 262-ticker universe in data/fmp_tickers.csv maps to this structure.

Layer 1 — Money / Yield Curve (the upstream price-of-credit input)

Names (not tickers, but reference series): UST 2y, UST 10y, UST 30y, MBS-Treasury OAS, FHLB advance rate, OIS, fed funds futures (CME FedWatch). Numeric anchors: 30yr UST 4.96% (Mar 20 ref); 10yr UST 4.39%; MBS-Treasury spread 200-220 bps vs 160-180 bps historical; 2026 cut probability 3%. Direction this week: policy path widened (Factor 1−).

Layer 2 — Mortgage Rate / 30yr (the binding household input)

None of these archetypes is broken; the affordability ceiling is biting differently across each.

The signal localizes affordability stress to the FHA-leveraged FTHB cohort, not the general market. This validates the framework's Factor 5 weighting (0.25) being roughly half of Factor 1 (0.40): the affordability ceiling binds materially on a different cohort than the rate-lock-in releases.

Second-surprising-data-point: PHM's lower-end-of-range gross-margin commentary (April 24 print). This is consensus-underweighted because the headline ("2026 guide reiterated") reads bullish, but the GM-at-low-end signal says the mid-cycle squeeze has already started in the mix-shift cohort, not just entry-level. Risk 2 — Warsh delivers on removing forward guidance / dot plot before MBS spreads compress

  • Falsifiable threshold: First post-Warsh-confirmation FOMC press conference (likely July 30, 2026) explicitly removes the dot plot and forward guidance from the SEP. Independent of cut path.
  • Mechanism: Forward guidance has been a key channel through which MBS-Treasury spread tightens. Removing it raises the term premium on long Treasuries even at unchanged spot rates. MBS spreads stay 200-220 bps; the 30yr stays >6%; the Factor 1 unlock catalyst pushed out by 6-12 months independent of what core PCE prints.
  • Watch trigger: Warsh transition (June 2026); first SEP under his chairmanship (July 30 FOMC); any pre-meeting WSJ trial balloon on dot plot removal.
  • Thesis impact if fires: Material on Factor 1. The path arithmetic to 5.5% becomes meeting-by-meeting and high-variance; long-duration thesis names (mortgage originators, title insurers) face multiple compression on path uncertainty; apartment REITs continue to outperform.

  • Thesis impact if fires: Modest on the broad thesis; substantial on the LGIH-style entry-level builder cohort. LGIH continues to compress while TOL holds firm. Cohort bifurcation becomes structural; the "relief from rates falling" doesn't reach the marginal entry-level buyer. Apartment REIT short thesis weakens further (involuntary renters become structural, not cyclical).

Underappreciated catalyst (multi-paragraph + time-to-tape tag)

VantageScore 4.0 + FICO 10T historical loss-rate distributions, due summer 2026

Time-to-tape: 60-120 days (loss-rate publication summer 2026; lender adoption signaling 2026 H2-2027 H1).

The April 22 FHFA + HUD authorization of VantageScore 4.0 and FICO 10T is being framed by mainstream coverage as a credit-scoring competition story. Three pieces of evidence consensus is not connecting that make this a Factor 4 catalyst:

1. VantageScore 4.0 incorporates rent-payment history into scoring. Per the FHFA April 22 release and Brownstein analysis, VantageScore 4.0 has been engineered to incorporate non-credit data including rent history. Industry estimates (per VantageScore's own white papers and McKinsey analyses) suggest this expands the qualifiable-borrower pool by 4-7M consumers — primarily renters who've been paying on time but have no traditional credit footprint to quantify.

2. Pulte's "stay tuned" on LLPAs. FHFA Director William Pulte signaled at the April 22 release event that GSE Loan-Level Price Adjustments (LLPAs) for new-cohort loans (those scored on VantageScore 4.0 or FICO 10T) may be priced differently — accretively — for lenders who adopt early. The implication: GSEs are reserving the option to price the new-cohort loans more favorably, creating a pricing incentive for early lender adoption.

3. Historical FICO 10T data publishes summer 2026. FICO 10T has historical loss-rate distributions that haven't been published yet but are due summer 2026 per the FHFA timeline. If the loss-rate distributions show parity with FICO Classic (i.e., FICO 10T loans don't default at higher rates), lenders adopt because LLPA pricing is accretive.

Why consensus is missing this: Mainstream credit-scoring coverage focuses on the headline competition between VantageScore and FICO; the read-through to housing demand is treated as ancillary. But the second-order effect — 4-7M new qualified borrowers in 2026 H2 / 2027 H1 — directly attacks the demographics ceiling (Factor 4) at exactly the moment when entry-level cohort qualification is binding hardest. The cohort that's currently driving LGIH's 45.6% cancel rate is precisely the cohort that VantageScore 4.0 + rent-payment-history scoring is designed to absorb.

Citable consensus that misses this: Bloomberg's coverage of credit-scoring competition frames it as a fee-and-vendor story; WSJ's coverage similarly misses the Factor 4 read-through. Neither connects rent-history scoring to the FHA-cohort demand recovery.

Citable data point that supports this: Per Federal Reserve Survey of Consumer Finances (2022), there are ~26M renting households whose median income is materially above what their reported FICO scores would qualify them for. Even if 15-20% of these are absorbed by VantageScore 4.0 + rent-history scoring, the math on Factor 4 ceiling shifts materially.

The trade-relevant insight: This catalyst fires before the Factor 1 unlock. If summer 2026 loss-rate data is favorable, the LGIH-cohort affordability stress eases via the demand side (more qualified buyers) rather than the supply side (lower rates). The implication for the Tier-1 builder long basket: DHI / LGIH / KBH — entry-level FTHB-leveraged builders — are set up for a non-rate-driven catalyst that consensus is ignoring.
